This additional practice time is to be done in a guided group retreat format. This can be done in a location convenient for the student. items.title: How does mindfulness need to be adapted to be safe for people experiencing psychosis? Would you feel comfortable incorporating mindfulness into your work with a client with psychotic symptoms? How would you do it? There is now sufficient research and clinical experience to encourage careful clinical practice of adapted mindfulness interventions for people struggling with psychotic experiences. But research has yet to examine whether any clinical improvement is mediated by mindfulness skills, acceptance of psychosis and self, or metacognitive insight. It is also unclear if, why and how mindfulness can reduce the perceived omnipotence of distressing voices. Given what we know and don’t know would you incorporate mindfulness into working with clients experiencing psychosis and how?

items.title: I didn't say my thoughts, but I shift between the intersecting ideas of critical race theory (from Fanon forward) and political post modern skeptical views (Foucault forward) about how much change can actually occur in power structures and thinking in societies to moments of more hopefulness. We all can try to find the places we can use any power and privilege to effect change most effectively.

items.title: I wanted to share this video from my colleague, Francis Kaklauskas, that is related to this weeks discussion and hope I covered something relevant. What is discussed about can be found in recent reviews from neuroscience experiments about predictions, perception, and emerging ideas about the dimensionality of psychosis in everyone. I listened to it back as I did the dishes, and worried a point at the end could have been clearer. I am talking about our ideas of hope and predictions for change in terms of racial justice is influenced by our experiences.

items.title: Carlos doubts his own perception and bodily awareness, frequently questioning whether he would “even know” if he hit his head. These behaviors—particularly the compulsive reassurance seeking—are interfering with his daily functioning, particularly his social and emotional well-being.
items.detail: from: Anxiety Disorders Case Conceptualization and Discussion

items.title: Carlos is a 24-year-old Latino male with OCD characterized by obsessional doubt regarding head injury and compulsive reassurance seeking. His symptoms are maintained through cognitive distortions, behavioral reinforcement, family accommodation, and stalled developmental autonomy. An integral AQAL approach incorporating exposure and response prevention cognitive restructuring, family psychoeducation, identity work, and systemic independence planning offers the most comprehensive intervention.
items.detail: from: Anxiety Disorders Case Conceptualization and Discussion

items.title: Carlos presents with a persistent, distressing fear of hitting his head and getting a concussion. This fear has been ongoing since a concussion he experienced during high school football and is exacerbated by past incidents of hitting his head (e.g., bumping into low doorways).

items.title: New counselors often fear that asking directly about suicide may have a negative impact on the client or the counseling relationship, a myth that often hampers prevention efforts. It is vital that new counselors explore their reactions to working with suicidal clients before they are faced with a client in crisis so that they may respond in a caring and competent way.

items.title: Due to the difficulty of addressing addictions substance use treatments have necessitated innovations in behavioral health. This field has had to go beyond the traditional medical model of treatment to focus on treating the whole person. This week’s discussion goes beyond the normative treatment approach and presents several unique perspectives on the treatment of substance abuse. Phenomenological data is presented on the experiences of individuals who underwent mindfulness based therapy for individuals with co-occurring disorders, natural recovery from methamphetamine use, and ibogaine treatment for opioid use.

items.title: This week we will move on to substance use and substance related disorders. Over the past few years I have consulted on several research studies featuring unique perspectives on substance abuse treatment that I would like to share with you. I would like to challenge you to think integratively about substance use from an AQAL perspective, and like many other disorders, issues and phenomenon involving mental health we have covered this semester, societal thinking about substances has changed over the years. From the social systems quadrant, what constitutes legal and illegal substances has changed as more states are decriminalizing certain drugs and legalizing marijuana for both medical and recreational use. Currently research is being conducted at universities and medical centers such as John Hopkins on the use of hallucinogens such as psilocybin and ibogaine to treat certain addictions. Such research and resurgence in the therapeutic use of hallucinogens is leading to their declassification from Category 1 drugs. These are interesting times for those studying substance use and abuse.

items.title: Effective in task completion process Provides a sense of direction and purpose Setting a target and reaching that target—self-reliance, self-trust, self-confidence, improve outlook, feel fulfilled in the counseling work. Mapping direction and progress Establish milestones to monitor direction and progress of the healing process Increase hope and motivation Hope for the future—provides clear vision and direction in where to go—can be energizing to fulfill a dream Can more effectively mobilize self to action, both in therapy and outside of it Contribute to a sense of client autonomy Can provide greater empowerment and autonomy—encourages clients to face what may be difficult, overcome demoralization, accept what is, and enhance a sense of personal mastery Provide structure Especially helpful for clients who don’t have a full sense of what to do or where to go Knowing where the client is, and where they hope to go—gives structure to begin conceptualization strategies and plans for goal achievement Serve as a prescriptive Allows client and counselor to know when contract has been met or where progress is lacking and perhaps a referral is in order ACA Code of Ethics (A.11.b) If counselors are unable to assist clients, they avoid entering or continuing counseling relationships. Counselors are knowledgeable about culturally and clinically appropriate referral resources and suggest these alternatives. SMART goals—therapeutic value and utility We don’t want to create goals that are impersonal, vague, overly generalized, or unrealistic--- not as effective and may increase frustration, hopelessness, and diminish self-esteem Other ways to think about goals: gainful, optimistic, appropriate, legitimate, and simple SMART GOALS: Specific Provide needed direction and clarity—clear direction, purpose, and markers for progress Ex unclear goals: I want to feel better, I want to do better in my relationship with my partner, I want to be happy. It is our task to help clients’ reformulate goals so that they exhibit the characteristic of specificity. Measurable When goals are specific, we can measure the progress made along the continuum of what is to what is desired Some goals are easy to quantify if they involve specific behavioral markers or assessment measures Goals that reflect internal states (e.g., feeling less stressed out, feeling more self-esteem) may require the creation of a subjective goal scale Draw a horizontal line--- what is to what is desired and create criteria together (ex: disappearance of depressive symptoms) Attainable Need to be achievable given client’s resources, capabilities, and current circumstances Also need to be reasonably challenging and require client to stretch themselves, while also being achievable (c.f. flow states) Result-oriented Considering the impact of achieving the specific goal and its results—helping clients to see this impact as it affects their life and the lives of those around them-- motivator Time-bound Have a clearly defined target date—increase motivation and allows client and counselor to measure progress and adjust strategies as need be Reasonable, flexible, and also not unnecessarily lengthy where it might result in less motivation, nor create more stress with being unnecessarily short Factors to consider in setting priorities for addressing multiple goals Asking clients to reflect on questions such as: Why should I pursue this goal? Is it worth it? Is this where I want to invest my time, money, and energy? What competes for my attention? What may be gained from pursuing this goal? Can begin with the problem that is causing the client the most pain Client-directed. Client must have interest in working on the goal. If client is not aligned, it is important to work on building the therapeutic relationship while examining reasons counselors believes it is important to investigate while inviting client to share their perspective. Cost-effective goal attainment when beginning (reasonable to achieve with biggest impact for least amount of cost—psychological, social, physical)—strengthen relationship and provide self-efficacy to move to more difficult areas. Challenges to the creation of therapeutic goals Sometimes complex and intricate goals need to be presented into a series of smaller goals. For some clients (e.g., mandated, low motivation) the goal may be to increase awareness and ownership of the need to change. Goals can help them see the benefits of change over the costs of the present situation. Clients who are concerned about ability to formulate or reach a goal and prematurely limit self in creating one: miracle0question (Berg and Miller, 1992). Future oriented question, invites client to envision a future time when their world is exactly as they wish it to be—invites client to think outside of the box, expand view of what is desired without concerns of constraints. Suppose when you fall asleep tonight and the entire house is quiet, a miracle happens. The miracle is that the problem that brough you here is solved. However, because you are sleeping, you don’t know that the miracle has happened. So when you wake up tomorrow morning, what will be difference that will tell you that a miracle has happened and the problem that brought you here is solved (de Shazer, 1985, p.5) Emphasis on a changed self or a changed context Allows for expansive dreaming which may be an excellent starting place for the formulation of meaningful and useful goals Client’s confusing strategies with goals May restrict avenues attainable for goal acheivement Ex: “I would like to be find a partner and be in a relationship.” May actually be a strategy to achieve some other goal. “Let’s assume you have a partner. How would that work for you?”—seeing it as a step to some other outcome the client is seeking. What is this a pathway towards? What are the underlying desires? Can open up additional avenues.

items.title: Telehealth, e-therapy, online therapy, online counseling, tele-medicine, e-counseling, or TeleMental health: all refer to the use of digital technology to provide clinical services, such as assessment and treatment. Telehealth activities may include providing clinical services by telephone, email, chats, interactive televideo communications technology such as Skype, or via virtual reality (VR) such as Second Life (SL), to individuals in conjunction with face-to-face (f2f) therapy or with no in-person contact. States and organizations vary in their definitions of telehealth.

items.title: Dr. Marina Dorian is an associate professor in the Mindfulness-Based Transpersonal Counseling Program at Naropa University. As an experienced psychologist, meditation instructor, and contemplative educator, she brings a wealth of expertise in clinical issues, mindfulness practice, and relationship dynamics to her teaching and research. Dr. Dorian earned her doctoral degree in clinical and community psychology from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign and previously served as a faculty member at the California School of Professional Psychology at Alliant International University.

items.title: Shyamaa Marie Creaven is a licensed professional counselor, a certified Gestalt therapist, and a body-centered trauma specialist who has been delivering spiritually-integrated psychotherapy since 2004. She received her Gestalt Certification (2004) from the Gestalt Institute of the Rockies after having lived and studied at Esalen Institute in California. She has offered courses at The Gestalt Institute of Cleveland and is working on a publication for the International Gestalt Journal on Process Theology and the Cycle of Experience. Shyamaa has taught clinical skills, Gestalt therapy theory, trauma theories, and MDiv courses on spiritual care at the BA and MA levels since 2006. Raised in a half-way house in Southern California, one could say she has been in the mental health and human services field for much of her life in various capacities. She received her Ph.D. from the University of Denver and Iliff School of Theology in the Joint PhD program in Religious and Psychological Studies in 2022. Dissertation titled: A sense of trust: Somatic spiritual practices as a path to wholeness in intercultural spiritually integrated trauma care.

items.title: What if we risk telling the wrong story about human nature, capacity, and the real sources of growth out of suffering with our current "trauma-informed" lenses? This course will utilize polyvagal theory, existential psychology (gestalt, logotherapy, phenomenology), and mindfully-informed gestalt theory & methods to sensitively practice beyond the current strengths and blind-spots in a trauma-informed approach. Phenomenology philosopher and Psychoanalyst Robert Stolorow (2007) has argued that “pain is not pathology” (p. 10), stating that although the possibility of trauma is built into the very nature of human existence, every difficult and even terrible event does not rise to the pitch of trauma nor result in enduring symptoms. He has asserted that, to a great extent, people are well-designed to weather great difficulties through internalized relational supports (Creaven, 2022) and other key ingredients we will explore in this course. I am happy you have chosen to spend your time exploring this with me.

items.title: Mini Biography: I have been working as a Transpersonal Gestalt psychotherapist since 2007. I love holding deeply transformative space for my my clients, and teaching student therapists has become one of my deepest passions. As a visiting instructor at Naropa University, I strive to clearly articulate and authentically embody the transpersonal framework that I teach. I've published 4 books on transpersonal transformation, and I have a training institute where I offer ongoing training for therapists who want to deepen into this work.

items.title: The breath is part of the stress response. For example, when we are about to confront something frightening for us, a situation such as public speaking or a job interview, we might start having some negative thoughts (“I won’t do well”), and our breath will become shallow. We will not be exhaling completely, we will be breathing high up in the chest, we don’t get good gas exchange, and we start feeling worse. The tension and anxiety in our body increases a feeling of threat, we stop thinking clearly, and we have more negative thoughts. This vicious cycle increases the limited breathing pattern, the fear, and negative expectations. Research has shown a relationship between the way we breathe and our other emotions as well.

items.title: The course will emphasize lovingkindness (metta) and tonglen meditations, as well as contemplative compassion exercises appropriate for counseling.
items.detail: from: Course Overview

items.title: The development of compassion for oneself and others is explored in this course through practices of loving kindness (metta) and tonglen. Specific topics include the application of compassion meditation to the healing process; resilience in the counseling relationship; evidence-based skills development; and preventing burnout. Group sessions of meditation practice, discussions, and individual instructions are provided.

More is encouraged, and mindful movement practice is highly recommended before or after your sitting meditation session. If you have questions please discuss your practice schedule and aspirations with your meditation instructor.

items.title: This tool is useful not just with situations that are frightening, but many other situations as well such as when we are angry at someone and about to express our anger in an impulsive or otherwise ineffective way, when we are experiencing any emotion that is difficult for us, and when we are having trouble getting to sleep. Deep breathing increases relaxation and enhances a sense of well-being. To be able to use our breath for these purposes, we need to first learn and practice the specifics of diaphragmatic breathing. With regular practice, we are calmer every day and also able to use this skill more readily when under stress.
